sections.subtitle: Match Overview
sections.text: Fulham, sitting at 11th in the league with 51 points, host Everton, who are 14th with 39 points, at Craven Cottage in London. This matchup promises to be intriguing as Fulham look to leverage their home advantage against an Everton side struggling to climb the table. The referee, Darren England, is known for averaging 3.7 yellow cards per game and 0.13 red cards per game, which could influence the physicality of this encounter.

sections.subtitle: Recent Head-to-Head History
sections.text: Looking at the last 5 matchups between these sides, Fulham have not lost to Everton, with 2 wins for Fulham and 3 draws. Their most recent clash on October 26, 2024, at Everton’s home ground ended in a 1-1 draw, with Everton’s Beto earning Player of the Match honors with a Sofascore rating of 7.7. Historically, head-to-head games have been low-scoring, with 6 out of 7 matches featuring under 2.5 goals, and Fulham often being the first to score in 4 of the last 5 encounters.

sections.subtitle: Fulham’s Key Players and Form
sections.text: Fulham will rely heavily on Alex Iwobi, a midfielder in fine form over the last 5 matches with an average Sofascore rating of 7.52, 2 goals, and 2 assists. Raúl Jiménez, their forward, has contributed 10 goals this season against an expected goals figure of 11.20, showing consistency in attack. Defensively, Joachim Andersen stands out with a league rank of 5 for long balls as a percentage of total passes (10%), alongside a solid average rating of 7.07. However, Fulham are missing key players like forward Rodrigo Muniz, who has 8 goals this season, which could impact their attacking depth.

sections.subtitle: Everton’s Key Players and Challenges
sections.text: Everton have players like Iliman Ndiaye, a midfielder with a league rank of 4 for successful dribbles per game (2.1), who could trouble Fulham’s defense with his flair, having scored 7 goals this season. Beto, their forward, has also been effective with 7 goals against an expected goals value of 6.16, showing clinical finishing. Defender Michael Keane leads the league with 12.2% of his passes being long balls (rank 1), while Jarrad Branthwaite ranks 4th for ground duels won at 73.3%. However, Everton miss key defender James Tarkowski, ranked 5th for aerial duels won at 74.6%, which could weaken their backline.

sections.subtitle: Team Statistics and Trends
sections.text: Fulham boast impressive stats with 52.3% average ball possession (8th in the league) and lead the league in accurate crosses (222) and percentage of goals assisted (84%). They also rank 3rd for interceptions per game (8.06) and 5th for ground duels won (51.6%), showing defensive tenacity, though they’ve gone 7 games without a clean sheet at home. Everton, on the other hand, have a streak of 3 games without a win and 8 out of 9 recent matches with under 2.5 goals, indicating a struggle to find the net. Their goal distribution shows a tendency to score late, with 32.6% of goals coming between the 76th and 90th minutes.

sections.subtitle: Standout Historical Performer
sections.text: Fulham’s goalkeeper Bernd Leno has been a standout in the last 5 matches against Everton, boasting an average Sofascore rating of 7.78 and averaging 4.4 saves per game across 5 starts. While he hasn’t contributed goals or assists, his presence between the sticks has been vital in maintaining Fulham’s unbeaten run against Everton over these encounters.

sections.subtitle: Tactical Insights
sections.text: Fulham’s strength in creating big chances (73 created, 13th in the league) but missing 44 (6th least missed) suggests they convert well, ranking 5th with a 39.7% conversion rate. They also enter the penalty area frequently (29.8 per game, 6th in the league), which could test Everton’s defense, especially with the absence of Tarkowski. Everton, however, might rely on fast breaks (1 per game, 9th in the league) and late surges, given their goal-scoring pattern. Fulham’s tendency to concede first in 6 of their last 7 home games could give Everton an early edge if they capitalize.

sections.subtitle: Disciplinary and Set-Piece Outlook
sections.text: With referee Darren England’s average of 3.7 yellow cards per game, and considering Fulham’s 76 yellows this season (12th least) and Everton’s similar disciplinary record, we might see a cautious but physical game, as 9 out of 10 head-to-heads had under 4.5 cards. Fulham’s 5.54 corners per game (9th in the league) suggest set-pieces could play a role, especially against an Everton side that has struggled to keep clean sheets in their last 3 away matches.
